coredata.dc:title: Quantitative determination of redox-active carbonyls of natural dissolved organic matter
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ya
Ma, Jiahai
coredata.dc:description:
Natural dissolved organic matter (DOM) is ubiquitous in environment and plays an important role in numerous environmental processes. Although the molecular basis of the reactivity of DOM remains poorly understood due to its extreme complexity, redox-active carbonyls (aromatic ketones/aldehydes and quinones) within DOM are believed vitally important. Except the rough determination of total carbonyls (including non-redox active –COOR) based on inflexible 13C chemical shift range by expensive and time-consuming solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), there is no ready method to quantify redox-active carbonyls in DOM. Here we show that after treatment with sodium borohydride (NaBH4) by selectively eliminating redox-active carbonyls, quenched fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CD) by DOM recovered dramatically, and displayed a good linear relationship between redox-active carbonyls detected and DOM concentration (R
2 ≥ 0.977), thus allowing first quantitative determination of the redox-active carbonyls of DOM. Eight DOM isolates present 0.59%–0.90% redox-active carbonyls by the current method. And this method is robust from coexisting proteins and salts. This method could provide better or equal instructive results compared with solid-state NMR for total carbonyls or electrochemical method for electron-accepting capacities (EAC). Our results provide the underlying structural basis of many important geochemical processes that mediated by DOM. We posit that this method could apply to other complex molecular systems such as the atmospheric aerosols and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), too.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural dissolved organic materials
Redox-active carbonyls
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of redox active carbonyls in DOM and natural water sample
DOM’ reduction induced fluorescence recovery of CD
Quantifying carbonyls by reduction induced fluorescence enhancement

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence intensity of (a) the CD with 10 mg/L SRHA before and after NaBH4 reduction, inset: control experiment and fluorescence responses of CD to the variation of BSA with Excitation/Emission of 320/400 nm; (b) Fluorescence intensity of SRHA before and after reduction at different concentration, the ellipse part with blue corresponds to 10 mg/L SRHA; (c) Fluorescence intensity of CD with different concentrations, inset: excitation-emission matrix (EEM) spectra of CD, orange part in center is the maximum of fluorescence intensity; impacts of (d) pH, (e) ionic strength and (f) mental ions on the fluorescence of the CD with Excitation/Emission of 320/400 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence intensity of (a) CD with rising SRHA concentration before (original) and after (reduced) reduction; (b) Linear increase in Fred/Fori with increasing concentration of SRHA, Fred: fluorescence intensity of CD with reduced SRHA, and Fori: fluorescence intensity of CD with original SRHA; (C) Relationship between Fred/Fori and increasing concentration of the redox-active carbonyls from mixed model compounds, inset: fluorescence intensity of the redox active carbonyls from mixed model compounds before and after reduction at different concentrations, Fred: fluorescence intensity of CD with reduced redox-active carbonyls from mixed model compounds, and Fori: fluorescence intensity of CD with original redox-active carbonyls from mixed model compounds; (D) Linear increase in the concentration of redox-active carbonyls with increasing concentration of SRHA.

Linear relationships (a) between the concentration of redox-active carbonyls and DOM samples, yellow bars in SRFA represent error bars (n = 3); (b) Comparison the results obtained by this work with results estimated by 13-C chemical shift from IHSS; (c) Detection of redox-active carbonyls in SRFA and SRNOM mixed samples; (d) Detection of redox-active carbonyls in water sample. Comparisons between (e) Electron-accepting capacities (EAC) and (f) Our results of redox-active carbonyls. All data normalized to maximum value. Brown means terrestrial samples, and green means aquatic samples. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Quantitative determination of redox-active carbonyls of natural dissolved organic matter

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Natural dissolved organic matter (DOM) is ubiquitous in environment and plays an important role in numerous environmental processes. Although the molecular basis of the reactivity of DOM remains poorly understood due to its extreme complexity, redox-active carbonyls (aromatic ketones/aldehydes and quinones) within DOM are believed vitally important. Except the rough determination of total carbonyls (including non-redox active –COOR) based on inflexible 13C chemical shift range by expensive and time-consuming solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), there is no ready method to quantify redox-active carbonyls in DOM. Here we show that after treatment with sodium borohydride (NaBH4) by selectively eliminating redox-active carbonyls, quenched fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CD) by DOM recovered dramatically, and displayed a good linear relationship between redox-active carbonyls detected and DOM concentration (R
2 ≥ 0.977), thus allowing first quantitative determination of the redox-active carbonyls of DOM. Eight DOM isolates present 0.59%–0.90% redox-active carbonyls by the current method. And this method is robust from coexisting proteins and salts. This method could provide better or equal instructive results compared with solid-state NMR for total carbonyls or electrochemical method for electron-accepting capacities (EAC). Our results provide the underlying structural basis of many important geochemical processes that mediated by DOM. We posit that this method could apply to other complex molecular systems such as the atmospheric aerosols and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), too.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural dissolved organic materials (DOM), a complex mixture of heterogeneous organic molecules (Dittmar and Paeng, 2009; Kerner et al., 2003; Maizel and Remucal, 2017; Sutton and Sposito, 2005), originates from various biological precursor matrixes (Jiao et al., 2010; Leenheer and Croue, 2003; McCarthy et al., 1998; Ogawa et al., 2001; Trubetskoj et al., 2018; Yamashita and Tanoue, 2008; Zhao et al., 2017) and is the largest carbon reservoir (Cory et al., 2014; Nelson and Siegel, 2013; Wang et al., 2009; Zepp et al., 2007). The significant role of DOM are widely recognized in global carbon cycle (Arrigo, 2007; Catala et al., 2015; Hopkinson and Vallino, 2005) and other important environmental processes (Heller et al., 2013; Latch and McNeill, 2006; Liu et al., 2015; Quinn et al., 2014) with a typical concentration range of 0.2–20 mg/L in surface water (Rodrigues et al., 2009). However, with more than 50 years extensive research, the underlying molecular structure basis of DOM still remains unclear due to its extreme complex molecular structure and heterogeneity (Aluwihare et al., 1997; Benner et al., 1992; Dittmar and Paeng, 2009; Lehmann et al., 2008). In recent years, redox-active sites in DOM were investigated increasingly (Aeschbacher et al., 2012; Guo and Ma, 2014; Ma et al., 2010; Ratasuk and Nanny, 2007; Sharpless and Blough, 2014), and the vital role of redox-active carbonyls such as aromatic ketone/aldehyde and quinone functional groups in dominating physical and chemical properties and thus the environmental processes of DOM were recognized gradually (Baluha et al., 2013; Canonica et al., 1995; Maurer et al., 2010; Zhang and Blough, 2016). However, to date, no solid method could quantify redox-active carbonyls in DOM. While solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) as a reliable method could only roughly determine total carbonyls of DOM based on inflexible 13C chemical shift range (190–220 ppm) (Kevin A. Thorn, 1989), it couldn’t distinguish redox-active carbonyls such as aromatic ketones/aldehydes and quinones from redox-inert carbonyls such as ester groups in DOM. Moreover, NMR approach requires large amounts of samples (milligrams weight needed) and is relatively insensitive, time-consuming (hours), expensive, and difficult for on line or in situ determination. Thus, a novel quantitative method for more interesting redox-active carbonyls is strongly desired.

On the other hand, with selective elimination of redox-active carbonyl moieties by sodium borohydride (NaBH4) treatment (the original aldehyde, ketone and quinone groups were transformed to alcohol-like and hydroquinone-like groups), DOM from different origins all present a preferential and significant irreversible loss of visible absorption associated with remarkable enhanced and blue-shifted fluorescence emission (2- to 3-fold increase) (Ma et al., 2010); and the unique phenomena could be readily explained by the charge transfer (CT) model due to elimination of the electron acceptor moieties with the loss of the charge-transfer transitions and emission from local donor states that are no longer quenched. In this CT model, the optical properties of DOM originate primarily from intramolecular charge transfer interactions between carbonyl-containing electron acceptor moieties (aromatic ketone/aldehyde and quinone) and hydroxy-(methoxy-)aromatic electron donors via forming a vast number of donor–acceptor charge-transfer complexes (Del Vecchio and Blough, 2004). Thus, elimination of the acceptor moieties by reduction is predicted to produce a preferential loss of the visible absorption due primarily to the loss of the lower energy charge-transfer transitions and not from the direct loss of acceptor absorption. Simultaneously, enhanced and blue-shifted emission is also expected, due to emission from local donor states that are no longer quenched because of the removal of acceptors by NaBH4 reduction; therefore, the excited-state chromophores can return back to ground state more easily and successfully by emitting fluorescence due to fewer obstacles for charge transfer transitions.

Moreover, NaBH4 treatment of complex environment matrices has become a powerful technique for broad environmental applications (Schendorf et al., 2016), and has been confirmed by cross-disciplinary studies (Phillips et al., 2017; Phillips and Smith, 2015). For example, Smith and colleagues have provided convincing evidence that light absorption of brown carbon aerosols largely come from charge transfer complexes in themselves, and the aerosols exhibit exactly same optical properties with DOM after NaBH4 reduction (Phillips et al., 2017; Phillips and Smith, 2014, 2015. In probing the mechanism of the photosensitized degradation of phenols by humic substances (HS), borohydride reduction highlighted the role of aromatic ketones and quinones triplets in this process, too (Golanoski et al., 2012). Moreover, our works have extended this selective reduction method to environmental chemical reactions, and confirmed the central role that the aromatic ketone groups in DOM plays in aqueous photoproduction of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (Liu et al., 2016), reduction of Cr (VI) by Fe3O4@NOM magnetic NPs (Xie and Ma, 2019) and dissolution of zinc oxide nanoparticles (Zhang et al., 2019).

Consequently, we seek to quantify redox-active carbonyls of DOM by NaBH4 treatment based on the potential relationship between reduction induced fluorescence enhancement and the amount of redox-active carbonyl groups. Besides traditional organic fluorophores, new classes of probes such as quantum dots have achieved great success since 1998 (Lakowicz, 2006; Bruchez et al., 1998), and nanotechnology is changing the fluorescence paradigm somewhat. Moreover, carbon quantum dots (CD) with strong emission may magnify the reduction-induced fluorescence change of DOM. In this work, after treatment with NaBH4, originally quenched fluorescence of CD by DOM recovered dramatically, and thus allows first quantitative determination of redox-active carbonyls of DOM. Our results overcame the limitations of existing methods, and provided a more sensitive, accurate, and time-saving approach to quantitatively determine redox-active carbonyls of DOM. And the current method is successfully used for a real water sample. The obtained results by this method are compared with solid NMR results provided by International Humic Substances Society (IHSS) and electron-accepting capacities (EAC) results in literature for 9 and 6 DOM isolates. Our method more precisely describes the more important redox-active carbonyls in DOM, which is distinct from the total carbonyls or EAC indicator. Meanwhile, based on the results by the current method, previous acknowledgement of the importance of redox-active carbonyls in DOM is undermined, too.

Ethylene glycol (Aladdin) and ethylenediamine (Alfa Aesar) were used to synthesize CD. NaCl (Beijing Shiji), HCl (Jingxi Shiji) and NaOH (Beijing Shiji) were used to study the effect of ionic strength and pH on fluorescence of CD, respectively. KCl (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), NaHCO3 (Aladdin), Mg(NO3)·6H2O (Alfa Aesar), FeCl3 (Tianjin Fuchen), ZnNO3·6H2O (Alfa Aesar), MnCl2 (Mackin), Al(NO3)·9H2O (Alfa Aesar), H2SO4 (Beijing Shiji), NaF (Beijing Shiji), CaCl2 (Beijing Shiji) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were used for assessing the method selectivity. SRHA (2S101H), SRFA (2S101F), SRNOM (1R101N), LHA (1S104H), ESHA (1S102H), PPHA (1S103H), NRNOM (1R108N) and UMRNOM (1R110N) were purchased from International Humic Substance Society (IHSS) and were formulated to stock solution (200 mg/L or 100 mg/L) for following quantitative experiments. Commercial humic acid (HA) (Sigma-Aldrich) was purified prior to use as reported previously (Chen et al., 2015). Briefly, the commercial HA was dissolved in a solution of NaOH (pH = 13.0) and filtrated, followed by the adjustment of pH to 7.0 with HCl and filtration; the precipitate collected by filtration was washed extensively with 0.1 M HCl, and freeze-dried. 4-Hydroxyacetophenone (4-HAO, Heowns, China), 4-Hydroxybenzaldehyde (4-HBA, Heowns, China) and p-Benzoquinone (p-BQ, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) were employed as model compounds. Mili-Q ultrapure water was used in this work. All chemicals were of analytical grade unless where noted.

The CD were readily prepared using a modified hydrothermal method reported by Hu et al. (2013). Briefly, 25 mL ethylene glycol was placed into a 50 mL poly-(tetra-fluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to environment temperature, 1.35 mL ethylenediamine was added into the obtained homogeneous solution. After stirring for 30 min, the mixtures were heated at 180 °C for 5 h in Teflon-lined autoclave. After cooling to environment temperature, the sample named CD was obtained.

Raman (532 nm; Renishaw inVia Plus, England) and FTIR (Bruker, Germany) were used to characterize the functional groups of the CD with air as the background. The sample spectra were scanned from 400 to 4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Diluted supernatants containing CD were dropped onto copper grids covered with an amorphous carbon film to prepare specimens for characterizing the morphologies and sizes of CD imaged by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). Fluorescence EEM spectra collected on the Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan) were measured at excitation wavelengths from 250 nm to 500 nm. Emission intensity was collected from 270 to 650 nm using a 0.1 s integration time and a 2 nm band pass for both excitation and emission monochromators by subtracting background (water). Fluorescence intensity for EEM spectra was collected in signal over S1/R1 formula. And fluorescence spectra measured at 320 nm excitation wavelengths with 2 nm bandpass and collected from 340 to 620 nm with bandpass of 2 nm. Fluorescence intensity was collected in corrected signal S1c formula with 3 accumulations averaged scans.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of redox active carbonyls in DOM and natural water sample
ce:section.ce:para: Before the measurement, the anti-interference capacity of this method was evaluated. NaBH4, pH value, and ionic strength were separately selected as the interfering substances and the impacts of BSA concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the CD were examined. Moreover, the impacts of common metal ions (Nikanorov and Brazhnikov, 2009; Zhou et al., 2015) on the fluorescence intensity of the CD were also evaluated.

To quantify the redox-active carbonyls, NaBH4 was employed, which selectively reduces the redox-active carbonyl groups (aromatic ketone, aldehyde, and quinone) in DOM (Tinnacher and Honeyman, 2007; Ma et al., 2010; Guo et al., 2014). In a typical redox-active carbonyls detection procedure, firstly, 80 μL of the as-prepared CD was added to 4 mL original DOM solution to give a final CD concentration of 2% (v/v). The optimal concentration of the CD was selected according to its fluorescence performance (Fig. 1 C). Then, the mixture was sufficiently shaken and the fluorescence of the system was recorded by a spectrophotometer. Secondly, repeating the first step after reducing the DOM sample using excess NaBH4 (∼20 times mass) for 2 h (Del Vecchio et al., 2017; Schendorf et al., 2019). Thirdly, the carbonyl quantity was calibrated by measurements of the mixture of small molecules with known carbonyl contents. The calibration curve used here was based upon 11 samples of varying concentrations. All fluorescence spectra used here were measured at 320 nm excitation wavelengths with 2 nm bandpass and collected from 340 to 620 nm with bandpass of 2 nm.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method, natural water sample was collected at an artificial lake (Yan Qi Lake, located in Beijing, China (40°40′N, 116°69′E)) in October 2019 and tested after standing for 24h. The standard addition recovery method was adopted to measure the redox active carbonyls in natural water samples and triplicate measurements were conducted for each sample. For example, the recovery of redox active carbonyls was calculated as follows:
(1) R = (Cf–Cm)/Ca × 100%
where R (%) is the recovery, Cm, Cf, and Ca (mg/L) are the detected redox-active carbonyls concentrations in the pristine sample, the measured redox-active carbonyls concentration after dosing a certain amount of standard redox active carbonyls into sample solution, and the quantity of standard redox active carbonyls added to the solution, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOM’ reduction induced fluorescence recovery of CD
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CD is nitrogen atom doped and highly hydrophilic (details in the Supporting Information (SI) as Figs. S1 and S2) and emits strongly with the peak around 400 nm (Fig. 1a and c and inset). DOM quenched the fluorescence of CD, this phenomenon was consistent with the previous work (Guan et al., 2017). As shown in Figs. 1a and 10 mg/L SRHA could quench 22.65% fluorescence of CD (2% v/v); and after the NaBH4 treatment, the fluorescence of the system recovered by 11.31% of original CD. Control experiments showed that the NaBH4 treatment could not enhance or decrease significantly the fluorescence intensity of CD (Fig. 1a inset), and confirmed that borate at relevant concentrations have no influences on the fluorescence of CD, too. Although the fluorescence intensity of SRHA itself increased a lot (245% at 10 mg/L and 206% at 20 mg/L, and 367% at 100 mg/L) after NaBH4 treatment, the SRHA intensity itself is far lower than the fluorescence intensity of CD at same conditions (same concentration, temperature, and treatment time). Moreover, as the concentration increases, CD emission intensity increases correspondingly and with at least an order of magnitude larger than DOM does; for instance, it’s 160,000 (arbitrary unit) for 2% CD fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1c), and ≤12,000 for 10 mg/L SRFA fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1b). Meanwhile, we also tested fluorescence intensity of HA before and after NaBH4 treatment, the maximum intensity is also far less than CD (maximum = 28340 at 20 mg/L HA, Fig. S3), so the fluorescence intensity of DOM does not interfere with that of CD. Thus, this dramatic emission recovery may serve as a ruler to measure the redox-active carbonyl concentration of DOM. And the fluorescence intensity of CD is robust, because pH, ion strength, common aqueous ions, and bovine serum albumin (BSA, a representative protein) do not have significant influence on it (Fig. 1 d-f). It is worth mentioning that our method is insusceptible to ion strength, but ion strength is troublesome for the solid-state 13C NMR method which usually requires desalination pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantifying carbonyls by reduction induced fluorescence enhancement
ce:section.ce:para:
Because of the robust fluorescence intensity at different conditions and dramatic emission recovery of CD with reduced SRHA (10 mg/L), the ruler role of CD to measure the redox-active carbonyls in SRHA was explored. Initially, we examined the fluorescence intensity of CD with different original and reduced SRHA dosages (0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 10, 15, 20, 50, 100 mg/L), respectively. We found that the system fluorescence intensity displayed dramatic recovery with reduced SRHA at every tested concentration (Fig. 2 a). And the recovered system fluorescence intensity (Fred/Fori) showed pretty linear relationship (R
2 = 0.989) with concentration of SRHA (a wide detection range with 0–100 mg/L) (Fig. 2b), while the relationship between Fred/Fori and concentration of SRHA itself was chaotic (Fig. S4). It means that the enhanced fluorescence intensity of SRHA without CD cannot be a ruler to quantify redox-active carbonyls in SRHA. To further quantify the redox-active carbonyls in SRHA, mixture of small molecules with known carbonyl contents was selected as a reference standard. 4-hydroxyacetophenone (4-HAO), 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde (4-HBA) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were respectively selected as ketone, aldehyde and quinone moieties, and their impacts on the fluorescence intensity of CD before and after reduction were explored. Not only there was no interference (fluorescence intensity of the mixture is quite lower than CD does, Fig. 2c inset), but also pretty apparent exponential relationship (R
2 = 0.996) between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of redox-active carbonyls were observed (Fig. 2c). Thus, according to the reference standard, an excellent linear relationship (R
2 = 0.994, intercept = 0) between concentrations of redox-active carbonyls and SRHA was obtained, and we could obtain the content of redox-active carbonyls in SRHA (Fig. 2d). Meanwhile, we also examined the influence of pH on quantification of SRHA by CD in the pH range of 4.0–9.0, and the fluorescence of the Fred/Fori was stable (Fig. S5). Therefore, the dramatic recovery of CD fluorescence intensity with reduced SRHA could serve as a ruler to measure the redox-active carbonyl content.

Redox-active carbonyls of another eight DOM were measured by this method. For each sample, the quantitative linear relationship was well matched between redox-active carbonyls and sample concentrations (all R
2 >0.975, Fig. 3 a). And this highlighted the robustness of the current method again. The redox-active carbonyl contents of eight DOM were 0.9% for ESHA, 0.7% for SRFA, 0.7% for SRNOM, 0.6% for PPHA, 0.6% for LHA, 0.6% for SRHA, 0.6% for UMRNOM, 0.5% for NRNOM, and 1.2% for HA, respectively (Fig. 3b). Compared with the total carbonyl concentrations (including both redox-active and redox-inert carbonyls) estimated by the 13-C chemical shift of solid-state NMR (Fig. 3b), the measured values by the current method was reasonably smaller; worthy of note, for the quantitative unit, our results are w/w% while IHSS results are carbon%.

In addition, using calculations of our data, the redox-active carbonyls in DOM are between 0.6 and 1.2 wt percent (Fig. 3). This range represents about one carbonyl carbon atom among 60 monocyclic aromatic rings, assuming DOM is all consisted of monocyclic aromatic ring. Actually, this value should be much less than 60, because DOM is not all monocyclic-aromatic ring structure but also contains abundant aliphatic structures (Perminova et al., 2018). Although it is not “about one ketone group per monocyclic aromatic ring was determined for both humic and fulvic acids” (Leenheer et al., 1987), these groups have significant contributions regarding active species production (
O 2
. −
, HO ·
, 3
D O
M ∗
) (Canonica et al., 2000; Chen et al., 2009; Lester et al., 2013) and disinfection byproducts formation (Lavonen et al., 2013) in water. Consequently, it is urgent to establish a more rapid and accurate detection method for redox-active carbonyls of DOM to promote the further development of related fields. On the other hand, the current method is almost immune to salinity, while the approach based on solid-state 13C NMR spectroscopy is sensitive to the salt content of the sample. Specially, samples with low salt content take about 10 h, and those with high salt content take about 20 h for NMR experiments.

To further verify the robustness of our method, we performed the DOM mixing experiments. Different amounts of SRFA and SRNOM were mixed, and the standard recoveries were 95%, 103.9% and 93.2%, respectively (Fig. 3c). Moreover, one real water sample was measured, too. SRFA and two mixtures of small molecules with known carbonyl contents were added to that water sample, and the recoveries were 107.1%, 102.7% and 98.0%, respectively (Fig. 3d).

Above results confirmed that this method could reliably quantify the redox-active carbonyls of DOM. Since no previous works could quantify the redox-active carbonyls of DOM, thus leading to lack of the reference standard; comparing the current results with related data would gain insight into the of redox-active carbonyl functional groups in DOM. Thus, we compared the current results with reported EAC data (i.e., the number of electrons that can be transferred to DOM at a given potential) to gain some insight into the roles of redox-active carbonyl functional groups in DOM (Aeschbacher et al., 2010). Worthy of note, different electron-mediators were used in these two studies; our method is functional group selective by chemical reagent NaBH4, while EAC is voltage selective by mediated electrochemical reduction (MER) using diquat organic radical to facilitate electron transfer between DOM and electrodes. Thus, for the six terrestrial and aquatic DOM isolates (tested by both methods), we make plot on a normalized scale (to the value of ESHA) in Fig. 3e and f.

From the comparison results of two methods, there is a clear underestimation of redox capacities and thus environmental activities of aquatic DOM (SRNOM, SRFA and SRHA) for EAC method (Fig. 3e). The current method had higher normalized value than EACs (Fig. 3f); this is particularly evident when comparing SRNOM and SRFA (Fig. 3e and f). Worthy of note, the EACs were quantified by MER, which was carried out at Eh = −0.49 V based on the potential range reported for various quinones (benzo-, naphtha-, and anthraquinones), thus supporting the hypothesis that quinones are the predominant redox-active moieties in DOM. However, a significant pool of moieties of DOM after electro reduction that could not be re-oxidized by O2 (revealing electron transfer to DOM was significantly irreversible) (Aeschbacher et al., 2010). In our opinion, the significant irreversible portion mentioned in previous studies is very likely to be aromatic ketone groups; thus, the deviation from quantified carbonyls here and EACs is due to the portion of aromatic ketones.

Without considering the contribution of aromatic ketones (i.e., considering quinones alone), appropriate understanding the environmental reactivities of DOM may not be achieved. Thus, the current method measuring redox active carbonyls of both quinones and aromatic quinones has great potential and provide additional evidence that aromatic ketones are probably the major redox active groups in DOM, while quinones do not.

We established a novel method that could quantify the redox active carbonyls in DOM, and the method is competitive to other related methods regarding the sensitivity, accuracy and efficiency. Previous estimates of total carbonyls originate from expensive solid-state NMR, while our study transfers the analyzing target to more specific redox-active carbonyls and has accuracy over the inflexible 13-C chemical shift range for complicated DOM. In addition, this rapid and simple method, based on DOM’s reduction induced fluorescence recovery of CD, is universal and not exclusive to DOM. We believe that this method applies to other complex molecular systems such as atmospheric aerosols and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) readily.

And our findings also intimate that the quantity and the contribution of those redox-active carbonyls in aquatic DOM is undermined in previous literature. For example, it was found an up to 0.8 mg/L (normalized value) redox-active carbonyls in SRFA, contrasting to a 0.34 mmol e/g for EAC. The abundance of redox-active organic moieties (electron shuttles) suggests that DOM could dissolve minerals, produce ROS and degrade small organic compounds more significantly than previously considered.

This study extends our understanding of the nature of redox-active carbonyls in DOM, raising concerns regarding DOM’s activity and potential of many environmental processes and shedding more light on the vital groups in DOM. Further, the obtained results will provide solid structural basis for a better understanding of the important environmental processes dominated or affected by DOM on both molecular level and geological timescale.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants 41573115 and 21377126) and Science and Education Integration Foundation (Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of sciences).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.watres.2020.116142.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Aeschbacher et al., 2012
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aeschbacher, M., Graf, C., Schwarzenbach, R.P. and Sander, M., 2012. Antioxidant properties of humic substances. Environ. Sci. Technol. 46 (9), 4916-4925.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Aeschbacher et al., 2010

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel electrochemical approach to assess the redox properties of humic substances

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aeschbacher, M., Sander, M. and Schwarzenbach, R.P., 2010. Novel electrochemical approach to assess the redox properties of humic substances. Environ. Sci. Technol. 44 (1), 87-93.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Aluwihare et al., 1997

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A major biopolymeric component to dissolved organic carbon in surface sea water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aluwihare, L.I., Repeta, D.J. and Chen, R.F., 1997. A major biopolymeric component to dissolved organic carbon in surface sea water. Nature 387 (6629), 166-169.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Arrigo, K.R., 2007. Carbon cycle - marine manipulations. Nature 450 (7169), 491-492.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Baluha et al., 2013

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective mass labeling for linking the optical properties of chromophoric dissolved organic matter to structure and composition via ultrahigh resolution electrospray ionization mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Baluha, D.R., Blough, N.V. and Del Vecchio, R., 2013. Selective mass labeling for linking the optical properties of chromophoric dissolved organic matter to structure and composition via ultrahigh resolution electrospray ionization mass spectrometry. Environ. Sci. Technol. 47 (17), 9891-9897.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Benner et al., 1992

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bulk chemical characteristics of dissolved organic-matter in the ocean
